title_content.content: An Intrinsically Temperature-Drift Suppression Phase-Locked Loop With MEMS Voltage Controlled Oscillator for Micromechanical Resonant Accelerometer

paragraph_content.content: , Jiawei Wang, Zheng Wang, Kunfeng Wang
, Zhaoyang Zhai, Pengcheng Cai, Xingyin Xiong, and Xudong Zou

Abstract—MEMS resonant accelerometers are robust to electromagnetic interference and have higher sensitivity and resolution. However, they are susceptible to temperature variation, which may limit this type of sensor used in high-end applications. This paper proposes a TDS-PLL with MEMS VCO for MEMS resonant accelerometers to be immune to temperature drift. The theoretical analysis of TDS-PLL MRA demonstrates it can suppress the temperature drift, which is verified by simulation and measurement results. Measurement results of the TDS-PLL MRA show that the bias instability is
full scale at room temperature. The temperature coefficient of bias stability in TDS-PLL MRA achieves
, which is lower 5.5 times than that of the traditional PLL MRA with frequency shift. [2022-0098]
Index Terms—MEMS VCO, phase-locked loop (PLL), resonant accelerometers, suppression of temperature drift.

MEMS accelerometers are widely used in consumer electronics, automobiles, aerospace and so on, due to the advantages of small size, low power consumption and compatibility with integrated circuits [1], [2]. Micromechanical resonant accelerometers (MRA) with frequency modulation signal output are less susceptible to external interference and have higher sensitivity and resolution, which is a good choice for high-precision applications.

Zheng Wang is with the Qilu Research Institute and The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Jinan 250132, China (e-mail: wangzheng02@aircas.ac.cn).

Xingyin Xiong is with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China (e-mail: xyxiong@mail.ie.ac.cn).
Color versions of one or more figures in this article are available at https://doi.org/10.1109/JMEMS.2022.3199486.

However, resonant accelerometers are susceptible to temperature variation, which is caused by temperature-sensitive in Young's modulus and thermal elastic stress of the material and residual stress [3], [4]. For example, temperature drift of Young's modulus in silicon material typically results in
temperature coefficient of frequency (TCF) of the resonator. In the cases of MRA devices for navigation application where the scale factor is about
[5], [6], [7], such TCF will induce an equivalent acceleration drift of
. It will introduce errors in some applications. Therefore, the compensation of temperature drift is an indispensable part of the high-performance MEMS resonant accelerometers.

The methods to suppress the temperature drift of MRA have been widely studied, which can be summarized as follows: novel mechanical structural design, temperature control and numerical compensation. Novel mechanical structural design usually employs passive methods to offset the TCF of the resonator, including introducing a temperature-dependent electrical stiffness design technique to compensate for temperature-induced frequency shifts [8], [9], [10], [11]; adding materials with complementary temperature properties to silicon to counteracts its inherent TCF [12], [13]. The above methods require special device structures or composite materials, and the fabrication process is complicated, while the tolerance of fabrication further reduces the temperature compensation performance. Another approach, namely, temperature control, has been used to provide temperature compensation for quartz crystal oscillators [14], integrated circuits (ICs) [15] and MEMS accelerometers [1], [16], [17], [18], [19]. It can measure the resonator's temperature and control the variation of temperature near the resonator in a small range through the micro-oven. However, the use of the oven needs a longer stability time and higher power consumption. In addition, numerical compensation is also one of the common temperature compensation methods for MRA [20], [21]. For example, a new temperature-sensitive unit is designed to measure the temperature of the resonator, and then the frequency drift caused by the temperature is calculated through the fitting curve [22], [23]; two resonators are designed and calibrated to obtain the relationship

page_footer_content.content: 1057-7157 © 2022 IEEE. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: KU Leuven University Library. Downloaded on January 19,2026 at 17:54:27 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

paragraph_content.content: curve between the two resonant frequencies and temperature and acceleration, then the acceleration is calculated through the measured two resonant frequencies [24], [25]. However, numerical compensation needs hardware unit for compensation algorithms implementation, which consumes more power and complicates the sensor system; Besides, the asynchronization between temperature measurement and compensation may cause hysteresis of drift compensation, especially in the fast variation of temperature or/and fast real-time compensation applications.

To solve the above problems, we propose an intrinsically temperature-drift suppression Phase-Locked Loop (TDS-PLL) with MEMS Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO) in this paper. An electrostatic tuning MEMS VCO with a micromechanical resonator, which has the identical geometry as the acceleration sensitive resonator (ASR), is used for locking and tracking ASR's resonant frequency shift induced by acceleration. At the same time, the resonant frequency drifts of both ASR and VCO's resonator (defined as temperature sensing resonator, TSR), resulted from temperature variation, are captured and formed negative feedback to dynamic suppress the temperature drift in TDS-PLL. If the ASR and TSR are in the same temperature field and have the same TCFs, the drift can be eliminated. Since the acceleration sensing, temperature drift capturing and suppressing are integrated into the TDS-PLL control loop, the system is intrinsically immune to temperature drift, which means the system completes temperature drift compensation at capturing acceleration. This characteristic of the TDS-PLL MRA may well work in the harsh environment, such as in fast variation of temperature applications. Besides, the TDS-PLL can directly convert the acceleration modulated frequency to voltage output and then easily digitalized by an ADC, which omit the complex FDC normally required for MRA sensor [26].

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section II shows the working principle and theoretical analysis of TDS-PLL MRA. Section III presents the system model of TDS-PLL MRA using Simulink and numerical simulations to verify the analysis. Experimental results are presented in Section IV, and the paper concludes in Section V.
The TDS-PLL MRA consists of five functional blocks: ASR, Phase Detector (PD), Proportional-Integral (PI) controller, MEMS VCO and Phase Shifter as illustrated in Fig.1. The output signal
generated by MEMS VCO drives the ASR after passing through the phase shifter. PD compares the phase difference between the output signal
proportional to the phase difference back to the PI controller. The phase detector, loop filter and MEMS VCO constitute a TDS-PLL.

According to the dynamic system theory of the PLL [27], when the resonant frequency of the ASR falls within the pull-in range of the PLL, the system can be locked automatically. Therefore, the system needs to meet the following conditions: (1) the natural frequencies of the ASR and TSR are close. (2) The phase shift around the loop is zero.

paragraph_content.content: As shown in Fig.2, a PLL is used to track the natural frequency of the TSR [28], [29]. From the resonant frequency expression

is negatively correlated with the bias voltage
applied to the resonant beam. Therefore, we can control the output frequency by varying the bias voltage
and realize the VCO based on the TSR.

The ASR is coupled to a mass by a lever to sense the external acceleration through frequency shifts, and its resonant frequency is affected by acceleration and temperature. The TSR is embedded in an independent loop, and its resonant frequency is affected by the bias voltage and temperature. Ideally, both resonators have the same frequency drift induced by temperature variance, so the error signal
which is proportional to the phase difference is not affected by temperature.

The MEMS resonator used in this work is shown in Fig. 3. It consists of a proof mass, force amplification levers and a resonator. The proof mass converts the acceleration along the sensing-axis into a force that is amplified by levers and

paragraph_content.content: 1) Mathematical Model of Nonlinear Resonator: A parallel-plate-driven clamped-clamped (C-C) resonator is used in this paper. It is shown that the micro-resonator can be easily driven to nonlinear region due to its size effect [30], [31]. Therefore, a comprehensive model including nonlinear terms is established [32].

paragraph_content.content: According to the Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion, the fourth-order polynomial needs to meet the following Equations (21) and (22) [28]. Under this condition, all the linearized averaged system's eigenvalues are asymptotically stable.

Substituting (19) into (21) and (22) can obtain the conditions that keep the system stable. When (21) and (22) are satisfied, it can be concluded that the nonlinear average system is asymptotically stable in the neighborhood of the equilibrium point. For a stable system, the initial frequency of the VCO is within a specific range around the natural frequency of the ASR. After the system starts, the TDS-PLL will adjust the excitation frequency to the natural frequency of the ASR.

title_content.content: B. Simulation of MEMS VCO and TDS-PLL

paragraph_content.content: As the excitation signal source for temperature sensing and frequency tracking in the system, the performance of the MEMS VCO directly affects the acceleration range, bandwidth and startup time of the accelerometer. Fig. 9 is the SIMULINK model of MEMS VCO, in which the white noise is added to simulate the system's regular operation. The simulation results show that the time for the loop to lock is related to the white noise. The greater the noise, the faster the loop locks, but it will bring frequency fluctuations.
The control voltage shown in Fig. 10 (a) is applied to the input of the MEMS VCO, and the output frequency is shown in Fig. 10 (b). When

, keeping the input voltage equal to zero, it can be seen that the PLL tracks the resonant frequency of the TSR within 0.03s. It directly affects the startup time of the TDS-PLL MRA. Then, by increasing the input voltage, the frequency can be reduced in real-time.

Fig. 11 shows the correspondence between the bias voltage and the frequency of MEMS VCO. Each time the voltage is increased (equivalent to applying a step signal), the output will oscillate for some periods to stabilize. The
curve obtained by fitting the simulation data. It can be seen that
is similar to the calculation result in TABLE I, then we can use the parameter to design the TDS-PLL.

When the system is working, the TDS-PLL tracks the frequency change of the ASR caused by the acceleration by adjusting the control voltage. For the acceleration signal shown in Fig. 13(a), the frequency output and voltage output of the system is shown in Fig. 13(b)(c). The system is finally locked and can detect the change of external acceleration in real-time, and the voltage output is negatively correlated with acceleration.
It can be seen from (25) that the TDS effect is related to the TCFs of the two resonators. Fig. 14 shows the frequency and voltage drift when the temperature changes from
. The frequency drift is only related to the TCF of the ASR. Therefore, no matter how
changes, the relationship between frequency and temperature does not change. If the two resonators have the same TCFs
, the system output voltage will be independent of temperature. In chip manufacturing, the tolerance of fabrication will lead to the deviation of the TCF.

The scale factor of the MRA is calibrated using a tilt table. The tilt table can precisely tune the tilt angle between

. This setup varies the gravitational component of acceleration along the sensitive axis, with the device response varying in proportion to the sinusoidal function of the tilt angle. At each tilt position, the output frequency and voltage are recorded by frequency counter and voltmeter for 30 seconds, and the mean value of the result is recorded.

at an averaging time of 1.6s and the bias instability of voltage output is

at an averaging time of 6s. The voltage output noise is greater than the frequency output due to the white noise in a high-frequency band. It takes a long time for voltage output to reach the lowest point, indicating the TDS effect.

title_content.content: C. The Test Results of Temperature Compensation

paragraph_content.content: To verify the TDS effect of the system, the accelerometer is placed in a temperature-controlled chamber which is heated to
, then the power is turned off to let the temperature drop, and the system output is recorded. The temperature drift is shown in Fig. 20. It can be seen that the temperature drift of voltage output
is much smaller than that of the frequency output
within 3600s. The temperature drift of both frequency output and voltage output is negatively correlated with temperature, because the ASR's TCF is smaller than TSR
The accurate temperature drift of voltage and frequency output over the range of
are shown in Fig. 21. The temperature coefficient of bias stability is
for the frequency output without compensation and
for the voltage output, an improvement of

. The residual error of voltage output which is nonlinear with temperature, approximating the previous theoretical derivation in (25). The residual error exists for two reasons: a) the difference in temperature coefficients between the two resonators, b) the two resonators are not on the same DIE, so they suffer from temperature gradients and stress.

page_header_content.content: MA et al.: INTRINSICALLY TDS-PLL WITH MEMS VCO FOR MRA

paragraph_content.content: This paper demonstrates a TDS-PLL with MEMS VCO for MEMS resonant accelerometer. The principle and theoretical analysis of TDS-PLL MRA shows it can completely eliminate the temperature drift. If the ASR and TSR are in the same temperature field and have the same TCFs. The influence of mismatch TCFs in ASR and TSR is also discussed. The simulation and measurement results verify the TDS-PLL can be immune to temperature drift. Measurement results of the TDS-PLL MRA show that the bias instability is
full scale at room temperature. The temperature coefficient of bias stability in TDS-PLL MRA achieves

, which is lower 5.5 times than that of the traditional PLL MRA with frequency shift. Besides, as a universal technique, our method can also compensate for any resonant sensor without affecting its original performance.

In the future, the temperature drift can be further suppressed by embedding two resonators on the same die and designing the frequency dividing circuit. In addition, higher efficient frequency tuning methods of the MEMS VCO can increase the full-scale range. For example, add an axial force modulation structure, use piezoelectric materials for frequency modulation, etc.

paragraph_content.content: Liangbo Ma received the B.E. degree from the School of Civil and Hydraulic Engineering, Huazhong University, Wuhan, China, in 2019. He is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology. His current research interests include micromechanical resonant accelerometer, digital closed loop resonator, and MEMS resonator interface circuit.

Zhaoyang Zhai received the B.E. degree from the School of Electronic and Information Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, China, in 2020. He is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Beijing, China. His research interests include micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) and MEMS inertial sensors.

Jiawei Wang received the B.E. degree from the College of Microelectronics Science and Engineering, Xidian University, Xi'an, China, in 2018. He is currently pursuing the master's degree with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Beijing, China. His current research interests include micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS), nonlinear dynamical systems, and MEMS resonator interface circuit.

Pengcheng Cai received the B.E. degree from the College of Microelectronics Science and Engineering, Nankai University, Tianjin, China, in 2016. He is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree in microelectronics and solid state electronics with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology. His current research interests include the measurement of resonant accelerometer and digital filter.

Zheng Wang received the B.Sc. degree from the School of Applied Physics, University of Science and Technology of China, in 2016, and the M.S. degree from the School of Microelectronics and Solid State Electronics, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2019. He was an Assistant Research Fellow with The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China, from 2019 to 2020. Since 2020, he has been an Assistant Research Fellow with the Qilu Aerospace Information Research Institute,
Jinan, China. His research interests include structure design, simulation and test of MEMS accelerometers, and MEMS mode-localized sensors.

Kunfeng Wang received the B.Eng. degree in measurement and control technology and instrument from Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, China, in 2017. He is currently pursuing the Ph.D. degree with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Beijing, China. His current research interests include micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS), resonant tilt sensors, and coupled MEMS resonator.

Xingyin Xiong received the B.Eng. degree in electronic information science and technology from the Minzu University of China in 2013 and the Ph.D. degree from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2018. He is currently working as a Research Associate at the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, The Aerospace Information Research Institute, Beijing, China. His research interests are in the areas of analog and mixed mode circuits and systems for MEMS inertial sensors.

Xudong Zou received the B.Sc. degree in microelectronics from Peking University, China, in 2009, and the Ph.D. degree in mechanics, materials, and design from the University of Cambridge, U.K., in 2013. He was with the Nanoscience Center, Department of Engineering, University of Cambridge, as a Research Associate, and the Churchill College as a Post-Doctoral By-Fellow. He is currently a Professor with the State Key Laboratory of Transducer Technology, Institute of Electronics, Chinese Academy of Sciences and the School of Electronics, Elec

trical and Communication Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. His main research interests lie in the area of integrated micro and nano-electromechanical systems, with a focus on resonators and inertial sensors, including the design and optimization of high resolution MEMS resonant inertial sensors, studying the nonlinear effects on the noise processes in MEMS oscillators, and the coupled MEMS resonators in sensing applications. He also works on the sensor fusion technologies, such as SLAM and VIO using MEMS sensors for autonomous navigation.
